query: clinical features of endometriosis including cyclical pelvic pain, gastrointestinal involvement, and infertility

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, some women suffering from endometriosis experience allodynia, a phenomenon when, even with the application of a nonpainful stimulus, an intolerable painful reaction is released. This condition indicates neuropathic pain. In the case of deep infiltrating endometriosis, the neural damage is caused by the invasion of endometrial stromal cells and mediators such as serotonin, histamine, prostaglandins, and nerve growth factor. These agents are released from mast cells, activated macrophages, and leukocytes, directly damaging the sensory nerve fibers and circulating in the peritoneal fluid. [7] Three subtypes of endometriosis often overlap with each other. These are the superficial peritoneal lesions, the ovarian endometrioma, and the deep infiltrating endometriosis. [20] Notably, the degree of clinical manifestations of the patient is not directly associated with the extent of the disease or the size of endometriosis lesions. [21] Patients with bowel endometriosis often present with a wide range of gastrointestinal symptoms like diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, or bloating and could mimic other clinical conditions like inflammatory bowel disease or irritable bowel syndrome. [22] Rectovaginal endometriosis is a very severe deep infiltrating form of the disease involving the vagina, the rectum, and the rectovaginal septum. Most often, it can be presented with bowel irritation, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, dyschezia, and rectal bleeding coinciding with menstrual bleeding. [23] Finally, a symptom with great concern is infertility, which often leads clinicians to suspect endometriosis, even in asymptomatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometritis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical manifestations and risk factors associated with endometriosis, such as dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Utilize appropriate diagnostic tools and assessments to screen for endometriosis in symptomatic patients. Tailor treatment strategies to individual patient needs, considering factors like symptom severity, desire for fertility, and comorbid conditions. Coordinate care and follow-up to ensure seamless coordination of care, referrals, and long-term follow-up to optimize patient outcomes and quality of life. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Dysmenorrhea -- Differential Diagnosis -- Gynecological Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis Obstruction of the reproductive tract: Imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum, vaginal agenesis, OHVIRA syndrome (uterus didelphys with obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal agenesis), and cervical stenosis Functional and nonfunctional adnexal cysts Adnexal torsion (this usually does not present with cyclic pain with menses) Adenomyosis Pelvic inflammatory disease/sexually transmitted infections Endometrial polyps Asherman syndrome Ectopic pregnancy Chronic pelvic pain Membranous dysmenorrhea: a very uncommon cause of colicky pain from uterine contractions with resultant shedding of the endometrium in one piece, which retains the shape of the uterus [61] Non-Gynecological Conditions (gastrointestinal, urological, and musculoskeletal) Irritable bowel syndrome Urinary tract infections Interstitial cystitis Musculoskeletal causes: abdominal wall muscles, abdominal wall fascia, pelvic and hip muscles, sacroiliac joints, and lumbosacral muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic pelvic pain is closely associated with endometriosis since endometriosis has been the cause in 71% to 87% of women suffering from chronic pelvic pain. [43] This disease can negatively affect the health-related quality of life and crucially hinder social, emotional, and sexual well-being as well as other domains like daily routines, family planning, efficacy, or productivity of patients in the working environment. [44] Additionally, patients with endometriosis have higher stress levels, report worse quality of sleep, and lower physical activity compared to healthy women. [44] Bowel dysfunction like constipation or other digestive problems can appear in females who have endometriosis as a result of the inflammatory process of irritation of the gastrointestinal system rather than due to the involvement of endometriotic nodules affecting the rectum since patients undergoing surgical treatment for rectal endometriosis might continue experiencing those displeasing symptoms. [45] Although primarily endometriosis was considered a benign condition, later research findings showed a higher risk for ovarian cancer in patients with endometriosis. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The disease diagnosis is usually delayed an average of 4 to 11 years from the onset of symptoms. This phenomenon occurs in low- and middle-income countries and wealthy societies with universal healthcare access. [19] This delay is attributed to the non-existence of a pathognomonic test or biomarker to detect the disease but also to the diversity of symptoms that could be considered physiologic responses during menstruation (like pain and discomfort), and the wide range of reported symptoms overlap with other gastrointestinal or gynecological causes. To diagnose endometriosis properly, the physician should start by taking a detailed history and performing a gynecological physical examination. Positive family history, pelvic pain, benign ovarian cysts, pelvic surgeries, and infertility issues enhance and alarm physicians to diagnose endometriosis. [19] Physical examination reveals variable findings, depending on the location and size of the endometriotic lesion. [28] Tenderness on vaginal examination, palpable nodules in the posterior fornix, adnexal masses, and immobility of the uterus are diagnostically indicating findings of endometriosis. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The most common symptoms of endometriosis are infertility and chronic cyclic pelvic pain; therefore, other conditions should be ruled out from the differential diagnosis. Chronic pelvic pain is a usual symptom derived from pathologic conditions of the urologic, reproductive, and gastrointestinal systems. Pelvic inflammatory disease, adhesions, endometritis, primary dysmenorrhea, and second dysmenorrhea due to adenomyosis, myoma, and cervical stenosis should be excluded from the pathologies of the genital system. [33] Other diseases that should be excluded come from the gastrointestinal system, like irritable bowel syndrome, constipation, inflammatory bowel disease, and the urinary system, such as interstitial cystitis or chronic urinary inflammation. [37] Finally, the physician should also rule out neurologic and psychosomatic causes leading to chronic pelvic pain. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The main complications of endometriosis include infertility or subfertility, chronic pain, and other debilitating persistent symptoms. These are wide in range, including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. Still, endometriosis can also cause a decrease in the quality of life of patients, complications of surgical procedures, anatomical abnormalities due to possible adhesions, bowel or/and bladder dysfunction, but in the case of ovarian endometriomas could even lead to the development of cancer. The role of endometriosis in infertile women has been debated for a long time, with literature research showing that infertile women are 6 up to 8-fold more likely to suffer from endometriosis than fertile females. [42] Even though several mechanisms have been proposed to explain this phenomenon, no consensus has been reached in the scientific community about the exact mechanism behind infertility in endometriosis. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic inflammatory disease is defined as an infectious inflammation of the upper genital tract in females. The disease can affect the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. Pelvic inflammatory disease is typically an ascending infection, spreading from the lower genital tract. The majority of pelvic inflammatory disease cases are caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae , Chlamydia trachomatis , and Mycoplasma genitalium ; bacterial vaginosis is also common. The diagnosis is made clinically in patients with cervical, uterine, or adnexal tenderness noted during a bimanual examination. Other findings also support the diagnosis, including purulent cervical discharge, cervical friability, fever, and positive test results for one of the common causative organisms. Pelvic inflammatory disease is treated with antibiotics. However, despite appropriate treatment, the condition is associated with several significant long-term complications related to pelvic adhesive disease, including ectopic pregnancy, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain. This activity reviews the pathophysiology, presentation, and management of pelvic inflammatory disease, highlighting the role of the interprofessional team in optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Endometriosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological condition characterized by the presence of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, typically at sites including the ovary, uterosacral ligaments, ovarian fossa, the pouch of Douglas, and the bladder, thought to be precipitated by genetic and epigenetic mutations that can predispose individuals to the disease, leading to infertility and immune system alterations. [1] [2] Contrary to the long-held belief that endometriosis is a progressive and recurrent disease, research indicates that most lesions are self-limiting due to fibrosis and immune response. Emerging research emphasizes the role of immune dysfunction, hormonal influences, and environmental factors in disease onset and progression. The condition presents with diverse symptoms, including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, but its progression and severity vary among individuals. Diagnosis remains challenging, often leading to delayed recognition, with surgical assessment via laparoscopy historically considered the gold standard. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Endometriosis ," for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: Dysmenorrhea -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopy is usually reserved for women who desire fertility and have suspected endometriosis as a cause of secondary dysmenorrhea.

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrioma -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: In general, patients with symptomatic endometriosis are frequently nulliparous females of reproductive age with a chief complaint of heavy or painful menses. Their periods will often last longer than seven days. They may complain of chronic pelvic pain, pain with sexual intercourse, or defecation (see a full list of symptoms below). Their periods are typically regular, though they may have shorter menstrual cycles (less than 27 days). The onset of pain for these patients is typically 2 or 3 days prior to the onset of their menses, and the pain typically begins to resolve a couple of days after their menses has started. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The peak of the disease incidence is greater in women between 25 and 29 years old and lowest in women over 44 years old, whereas the prevalence in whites is higher than in African-Americans. [18] The clinical presentation of the disease differs in women and may be unexpected not only in the presentation but also in the duration. Clinicians usually suspect and are more likely to diagnose the disease in females presenting with the typical symptomatology such as dyspareunia, namely painful sexual intercourse, pelvic pain during menstruation (dysmenorrhea), pain in the urination (dysuria), defecation (dyschezia), or infertility. [14] The pain is usually characterized as chronic, cyclic, and progressive (exacerbating over time). [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometriosis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for developing endometriosis and summarize the epidemiology of the disease. Evaluate the history and physical exam findings associated with endometriosis and describe the available ways to evaluate the disease. Differentiate the treatment and management options available for endometriosis. Communicate the importance of interprofessional team collaboration to improve the outcomes of endometriosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Pelvic Pain -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Gastroenterological: IBS, celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, colorectal carcinoma, and hernias.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Pathophysiology -- Endometriosis
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a chronic and painful gynecologic disease that typically presents with pelvic pain, AUB, dysmenorrhea, and/or dyspareunia. Dyschezia (pain with defecation) can occur if endometrial tissue is implanted in the pouch of Douglas. If the intestines or bladder are involved, abdominal pain and painful urination will occur, respectively. Symptoms typically vary with hormone changes and often get worse during menstruation. The inflammation associated with endometriosis has the potential to damage or scar the fallopian tubes and ovaries, leading to infertility. On physical exam, the uterus will be normal in size.

retrieved_docs.title: Gynecologic Pelvic Examination -- Indications -- Examinations to Assess Gynecologic Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: A pelvic examination is used to assess the external and internal female genital structures. The urethra, bladder, perineum, anus, and/or rectum are also frequently evaluated at the same time. [7] A pelvic examination is warranted to assess patients with relevant symptoms or concerns as follows: Abnormal bleeding, including menstrual abnormalities and postcoital and postmenopausal bleeding Pelvic pain, such as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, or dyschezia Abnormal vaginal discharge Itching Masses Visible lesions or ulcers Infertility Trauma Retained foreign bodies [8] Concerns regarding abnormal anatomy or development Neurological conditions Incontinence Prolapse or other pelvic floor disorders [9] Unexplained new gastrointestinal symptoms (eg, difficulty eating, early satiety, abdominal pain, bloating, etc) [7] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 827
retrieved_docs.content: ulcers. The framework links female infertility of cervical origin to اضطراب في عنق الرحم and to العقم لدى الإناث, and associates female infertility with endometrial implantation, reflecting the contribution of endometriosis to reproductive difficulty. Diabetic neuropathy is highlighted as a key risk factor for lower‑extremity ulcers, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring in diabetic patients. Overall, the integrated classification supports comprehensive documentation, risk assessment, and coordinated management across these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2855
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is connected to endometriosis through a broader relationship, indicating that endometriosis falls within the scope of disorders affecting the female reproductive system. Both Female Reproductive System Disorder and endometriosis are classified under the parent category اضطراب رحمي, which translates to “uterine disorder.” In this hierarchy, اضطراب رحمي serves as the overarching class, with Female Reproductive System Disorder representing a broader group of conditions and endometriosis representing a specific type of uterine disorder. This structure highlights how endometriosis is a particular manifestation of uterine pathology within the larger context of female reproductive system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 537
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is linked hierarchically to both اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder) and اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder) as parent categories, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system can be considered subtypes of broader pregnancy‑related and uterine conditions. Within this overarching category, specific diseases such as cancer of the uterus and endometriosis are included. Endometriosis is further classified as a type of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (female genital organ disorder) and also as a form of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), reflecting its position both within the general female genital organ disorder hierarchy and the more specific uterine disorder group. The hierarchical structure therefore connects pregnancy disorders, uterine disorders, and female genital organ disorders, with Female Reproductive System Disorder, uterine cancer, and endometriosis positioned as specific manifestations within this network. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding how various female reproductive conditions relate to each other and to broader reproductive health categories, supporting more comprehensive diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 552
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder and is also considered a subset of female infertility. Both cervical disorders and female infertility are targets for prevention and control strategies, as are peptic ulcers and endometriosis. Endometriosis is linked to female infertility, indicating that the presence of endometrial implants can contribute to difficulties conceiving. Pharmacologic management of endometriosis may include danazol, which has the potential to treat this condition. Implementing preventive and control measures for cervical disorders, peptic ulcers, female infertility, and endometriosis is essential for reducing disease burden and improving reproductive health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Manual conversion of position is a medical procedure whose site is the uterus, indicating that it is performed on or involves the uterine organ. Endometriosis is a disease that is located in the uterus, reflecting the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue within or on the uterine structure. Both concepts highlight the uterus as a central focus for therapeutic interventions and pathological conditions, underscoring the importance of uterine health in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad category encompassing various gynecologic conditions. It includes specific disorders such as endometriosis, which is classified as a type of uterine disorder (اضطراب رحمي). The category also relates to the parent condition اضطراب رحمي itself and to ovarian cyst (كيسة مبيضية). Recognizing that endometriosis falls under the umbrella of uterine disorders within the larger Female Reproductive System Disorder framework helps clinicians assess and manage these interconnected conditions more comprehensively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 992
retrieved_docs.content: This integrated framework aids clinicians in understanding the interconnections among various gynecologic conditions, facilitating more precise diagnosis, classification, and management of patients with disorders of the female reproductive tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a medical condition that falls within the broader category of اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (disorder of the female reproductive organs). This broader category is itself a subcategory of اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), indicating that uterine disorders serve as the parent classification for disorders of the female genital organs. Accordingly, endometriosis is also classified directly as an اضطراب رحمي, reflecting its nature as a specific type of uterine disorder. Understanding these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians recognize that endometriosis is both a uterine disorder and part of the wider spectrum of female reproductive organ disorders, which is important for accurate diagnosis, management, and patient education.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1207
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs are described as having a finding site in the ovary (ovarium), indicating that the ovary is the primary anatomical location where clinical manifestations of these inflammatory conditions are observed. Similarly, noninflammatory disorders of the ovary and general ovary problems are both noted to have the ovary as their finding site, emphasizing that a wide range of ovarian pathologies—whether inflammatory or noninflammatory—are localized to the ovary itself. In Arabic, the term “اضطراب مبيضي” (ovarian disorder) is also linked to the ovary as a finding site, reinforcing the concept that both the English‑language descriptors (inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, noninflammatory disorder of ovary, ovary problem) and the Arabic descriptor all converge on the ovary as the key site for diagnostic findings. This unified understanding underscores the central role of the ovary in the evaluation and management of pelvic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1182
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of fallopian tube and ovary, inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, and noninflammatory disorder of ovary all share the ovarium as their anatomical finding site. The ovary therefore serves as the common location for a range of gynecologic conditions, encompassing both combined fallopian‑tube‑ovary disorders, inflammatory pelvic diseases, and isolated noninflammatory ovarian pathologies. Recognizing the ovary as the primary site of these disorders is important for accurate diagnosis, targeted evaluation, and appropriate management of female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2427
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of the female pelvic organs are identified as having the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location where such pelvic inflammatory conditions manifest. Likewise, any ovary problem is described as having its finding site in the ovary itself, reinforcing the ovary’s central role in the pathology of disorders that originate in this organ. In addition, the ovary (ovarium) is noted to be the anatomical location for an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), highlighting that ectopic implantation of a gestational sac can occur within the ovary. Together, these relationships emphasize the ovary’s significance as both the site of inflammatory pelvic disease, the focal point for ovary‑specific problems, and a possible location for an ovarian pregnancy, all of which are important considerations in diagnosis and management of female pelvic health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1235
retrieved_docs.content: Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth and puerperium is regarded as a specific type of اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder). اضطراب الحمل, in turn, is identified as a parent category for Female Reproductive System Disorder, linking circulatory complications of pregnancy to broader reproductive system pathology. Female Reproductive System Disorder is also connected to اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder), indicating that uterine disorders serve as an overarching parent for conditions affecting the female reproductive tract. Endometriosis is positioned within this hierarchy as a concrete manifestation of both Female Reproductive System Disorder and اضطراب الأعضاء التناسلية لدى الإناث (disorder of female reproductive organs). Consequently, endometriosis is recognized as a specific uterine disorder that falls under the broader umbrella of female reproductive system disorders. Together, these relationships illustrate a nested structure in which pregnancy‑related circulatory diseases, uterine disorders, and general female reproductive organ disorders are interrelated, with endometriosis representing a distinct clinical entity within this integrated framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Dietoterapie is recognized as an allowed therapeutic approach for both العقم لدى الإناث (female infertility) and انتباذ بطاني رحمي (endometriosis). These two gynecological conditions are related, suggesting that dietary management may have relevance for the pathophysiology and treatment of each. Consequently, clinicians should consider incorporating diet therapy when addressing either العقم لدى الإناث or انتباذ بطاني رحمي, as dietary interventions could potentially benefit both interlinked conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory diseases of female pelvic organs, lesions of the ovary, and noninflammatory disorders of the ovary all share the ovary (ovarium) as their finding site, indicating that the ovary is the primary location for these diverse pathologic processes. In addition, the ovary can serve as the anatomical site of an ovarian pregnancy (حمل مَبيضِيّ), an ectopic gestation that occurs within the ovarian tissue. Together, these relationships underscore the central role of the ovary in both inflammatory and non‑inflammatory pelvic conditions as well as in rare pregnancy complications, highlighting the need for careful evaluation of ovarian involvement in female pelvic health and disease management.
